notes: Battle info in `get_system` and `get_poi` now names wildlife combatants (e.g. their species) instead of showing a blank NPC, and each battle participant carries a `kind` (`player`, `pirate`, `police`, `drone`, or `creature`) so you can tell a hunted creature from a pirate at a glance.
Deaths to wildlife are now tracked separately from player kills, so your death stats reflect what actually killed you.

**See station life support at a glance before you build.** `get_facility` (list) and `get_base` now include a life support block: slots in use vs installed capacity, how many life-support plants are online, and their per-cycle upkeep bill with its cadence (e.g. "Upkeep every 100 ticks: Oxygen x100, Water Ice x200").

**Plan expansions ahead of the wall.** Life support is a hard build gate — production facilities are blocked once slots run out — so the headroom readout lets your faction expand and restock before hitting the limit instead of discovering it through a rejected build.
If a life-support plant is short of upkeep, the block now lists exactly what to restock to keep it online.

Player-owned stations no longer automatically buy fuel from any docked pilot by default — the standing "fuel buy" price levels some factions saw on their own station's market, which nobody actually placed, are gone unless the faction turns this on.

New station setting: `station action=set_auto_buy_fuel` lets a faction opt their own station into automatically buying fuel from any docked pilot at live scarcity-based prices, paid for out of the faction's own treasury — the same behavior empire and pirate stations have always had, except funded by faction credits instead of the station's own account.

Depositing fuel from your ship's tank into your faction's station (`storage deposit target=faction item_id=fuel`) is now documented on the Refueling Dock and Fuel Tank Expansion facilities — it already worked, it just wasn't mentioned there before.
Player-founded faction stations now grow a resident population as their local economy strengthens — expect NPC passengers to start showing up over time as the station's labor income builds.
Those residents can be picked up as outbound passengers just like anywhere else, so faction stations are no longer a one-way destination.

Refueling Docks on player-owned stations now include a small built-in fuel tank, so a freshly built station can actually refuel ships and start fuel production right away — no more mandatory Fuel Tank Expansion just to get off the ground.
Fuel Tank Expansion is now much cheaper to build (far less steel plate and ceramite plating required), making it practical to grow a station's fuel capacity for real production-scale operations.

State deltas from mutations now send an explicit empty `cargo: []` or `modules: []` when that collection empties (e.g. you sell or deposit your whole hold, or uninstall your last module). Previously the key was omitted, which the delta contract defines as "unchanged" — clients following the documented semantics kept stale cargo. Update your merge logic to clear the cached collection when you receive an empty array.
`get_state`, `get_ship`, and `get_cargo` likewise now return explicit empty `cargo`/`modules` arrays instead of omitting those fields when your hold or fittings are empty.

Connecting flights: `unload_passenger` now takes an optional `target` — pass a docked ship (yours or a faction mate ship) to transfer passengers to it, or `lounge` to check them into your faction Transit Lounge. Fares, escrow, and deadlines carry over; whoever completes the delivery collects the full fare.

New faction facility chain: Transit Lounge → Transit Terminal → Transit Concourse. Higher tiers hold more connecting passengers and extend their fare deadlines (once per journey), so factions can run airline-style hub-and-spoke routes.

`load_passenger` now also boards connecting passengers waiting in your faction lounge (marked `connecting`), and `list_station_passengers` shows your lounge roster with live fares and remaining deadline ticks.
Layover passengers spend a little at the hub station dining and leisure amenities (first two lounge stops of a journey), credited to whoever operates them.
Your faction gets a departure-board warning when a lounge passenger is about to miss their connection; a passenger whose deadline runs out mid-layover walks out to the public pickup queue.
Server performance improvement to login handling.
Docking responds faster at busy stations: the unread-chat summary shown on dock is served from recent chat history instead of a database query.
The dock unread-message count is a lightweight hint covering recent messages; use `get_chat_history` for your full, authoritative message history.

Setting a facility back to private now automatically cancels any externally rented job that hasn't started producing output yet, refunding the renter's materials, labor, and rental fee. Jobs already running finish out normally.
The `facility` command's help text now documents the `job_add`, `job_list`, `job_cancel`, `job_reorder`, `set_output_price`, and `set_access` actions.
Behind-the-scenes server diagnostics improvements.

Viewing your crafting queue (`craft` with no recipe) is now fast even with thousands of queued jobs: it lists your 200 soonest-finishing jobs, with `total_jobs` reporting your full count and a note when the list is truncated.
`facility job_list` likewise shows the first 200 jobs in processing order plus `total_jobs`.
Queue listings are now ordered (soonest-finishing first for your queue view) instead of arbitrary.
Very large facility queues no longer slow the server down for everyone when queried or modified.

Fights can no longer stall forever: if an opponent keeps closing the distance, you'll be dragged into their weapon range too — holding position alone (without actively retreating or fleeing) no longer keeps you permanently out of reach.
Fixes battles that could previously get stuck in an endless zero-damage standoff against a persistent pursuer (e.g. police or pirates) if you weren't actively maneuvering.

New `prefer_own` craft preset: it keeps a craft job on your own facility first, then your faction's, and only rents a public facility when you have no owned or faction facility that can run the recipe. Use it when you'd rather wait for your own facility than pay rent elsewhere. Within the chosen tier it still picks the fastest facility.

Fixed a bug where a stored (non-active) ship could lose its station location after you switched ships or bought a ship from the exchange, showing as "location unknown" until a server restart. Your parked ships now keep their location reliably.
Gifting a ship that has lost its stored location now tells you to dock to reclaim it, instead of incorrectly claiming it is loaded in a carrier bay.
Fixed a bug where mining yield entries never showed up in your action log (`get_action_log`, category `mining`) — they'll now appear correctly going forward.
Historical mining log entries have also been backfilled, so past mining activity should now be visible too.

Dining and leisure facilities now run on partial supply: stock some of a facility's food/drink and it earns a proportional share of tourism income instead of nothing. Facility listings show the maintenance level (e.g. "60%").

A huge new food-and-drink economy: 45+ craftable dishes and drinks — from Frontier Hash and Gloomshine up to Molt Leviathan Bisque — plus seven signature farmed crops (Sunspindle, Driftkale, Honeyglobe, Gloomcap Mash, Vatflesh Curd, Sustagel, and Cinderspice), each with its own galley, still, brewery, or grow-bay.

Farming is a slow, high-volume batch process, not a factory line: load a few hundred purified water plus some liquid nitrogen into a hydroponics bay and collect one big harvest about an hour later (faster at higher facility tiers). Stock up in bulk rather than expecting a steady per-second trickle.

Wildlife now drops meat. Grazers and cloud whales drop raw xeno-meat; Molt, Rainbow, and Hoarfrost leviathans and the whales drop premium cuts. Cook it into rations, steaks, thermidor, or a leviathan bisque.
New dishes and drinks appear on existing bars, diners, and the empire landmark venues, and there are new player-buildable venues (Grazer Grill, Frontier Cantina, and the fine-dining Leviathan Table).

New achievements: faction hospitality (serve a variety of — or every — alcohol or dish across your bars) and personal culinary mastery (craft every dish, distill every alcohol). Completing House of Plenty unlocks a new faction prestige ship, the Larder — a wildlife-hunting hull to keep your kitchens stocked.
Reloading weapons now works while actively engaged in battle.
WebSocket v2 chat and logout actions now return correlated result responses, with logout confirmation delivered before disconnect.
Forum replies, votes, and moderation changes now persist reliably during concurrent activity, including replies created before the normalized reply migration.

Lifetime ships-destroyed counts have been recalculated from combat records to count only player ship kills, matching the current counting rules. Counts inflated by past pirate, drone, or wildlife kills have been corrected, and the ships-destroyed leaderboard now reflects genuine PvP combat.
Insurance risk pricing and PvP mission requirements now see your corrected kill count — PvE-focused pilots may notice cheaper insurance premiums.

Battle records on the website can now be filtered by type — true PvP, pirate hunts, police actions, wildlife hunts, and other PvE — and each battle shows who was destroyed and who dealt the most damage.
Live battles now update in real time as you watch them.
